title: Real-Time Gaming Leaderboard - System Design Interview
tagline: Design a low-latency monthly leaderboard with top ranks, user rank, and nearby competitors.
problem: Build a real-time gaming leaderboard where players gain points for match wins, tournaments reset monthly, all players are ranked, and clients can fetch the top 10, a specific user rank, and nearby ranks.

functional: Increment a player score when they win a match.
Show the top 10 players for the current monthly tournament.
Show nearby ranks around a user.
Reset or roll over leaderboards monthly.
nonFunctional: High availability for reads during tournaments.
requirements.designPrompt: The key educational move is comparing relational ranking to Redis sorted sets, then adding durability and partitioning only where the scale requires it.

capacity.label: Active leaderboard size
capacity.value: Up to 25 million players per monthly tournament.

capacity.label: Core operation cost
capacity.value: Redis sorted set updates and rank reads are O(log N).

capacity.label: Read patterns
capacity.value: Top 10 and current user rank dominate.

capacity.label: Core assumptions
capacity.note: 5 million DAU and 25 million MAU. Each active player plays about 10 matches per day. Score updates average hundreds of QPS and peak around a few thousand QPS. Monthly tournament keys bound the active ranking window.

capacity.label: Design focus
capacity.note: Estimate whether a single sorted set can hold the active tournament before introducing partitioning. Avoid premature sharding if memory and QPS fit.

dataModel.name: leaderboard_snapshot
dataModel.note: Periodic durable snapshot for rebuild and audit.

dataModel.name: partition
dataModel.note: Shard metadata when a leaderboard exceeds one sorted set.

steps.title: 1. Define the Score Event Contract
steps.description: A match result produces a durable score event. The leaderboard should be updated idempotently so duplicate match notifications do not double count wins.
steps.decisionPrompt: What is the source of truth for a score increment?

concepts.definition: An immutable record saying a user earned points from a match.
concepts.whyItMatters: It lets the leaderboard be rebuilt and audited.

concepts.definition: Processing the same match result more than once has the same effect as processing it once.
concepts.whyItMatters: It prevents duplicate scores during retries.

concepts.definition: A key that scopes scores by game and month.
concepts.whyItMatters: It makes monthly reset a namespace change instead of deleting everything immediately.

deepDives: Do not let clients submit arbitrary score increments directly without validation from the game service.
bottlenecks: Duplicate match events can inflate scores without idempotency.
Late events around month boundary need a clear tournament timestamp policy.
talkingPoints: Define whether ties share ranks before rank APIs.

steps.title: 2. Model Monthly Tournament Keys
steps.description: Each monthly tournament gets its own leaderboard key. Resetting the board means opening a new key and eventually archiving the old one.
steps.decisionPrompt: Should monthly reset delete data or switch to a new leaderboard namespace?

concepts.definition: A new logical key for each month.
concepts.whyItMatters: It makes reset instant and preserves historical boards.

concepts.definition: A retained previous leaderboard used for history or audit.
concepts.whyItMatters: It avoids destructive deletes at the boundary.

concepts.definition: A pointer from current tournament to its active namespace.
concepts.whyItMatters: It lets clients request current without hardcoding month strings.

deepDives: A namespace switch is safer than mass deletion because old data can still be queried or rebuilt.
bottlenecks: Late score events need month attribution by match completion time.
Archives should not stay in expensive hot memory forever.
talkingPoints: Make the API path include gameId and month, with a current alias as convenience.

steps.title: 3. Use Redis Sorted Sets for the Active Board
steps.description: A sorted set stores user IDs as members and scores as sort values. ZINCRBY updates a score; ZREVRANGE and ZREVRANK answer top and rank queries efficiently.
steps.decisionPrompt: Should the active ranking be computed in SQL or maintained in a sorted set?

concepts.definition: A data structure ordered by numeric score with efficient rank operations.
concepts.whyItMatters: It directly matches leaderboard update and rank queries.

concepts.definition: Redis command that increments a member score.
concepts.whyItMatters: It handles win events efficiently.

concepts.definition: Redis command that returns a member rank in descending score order.
concepts.whyItMatters: It answers user rank queries without scanning all players.

options.title: Redis sorted set
options.description: Use a hot in-memory sorted set for the current tournament.
tradeoffs: Very fast rank reads and updates.
Needs durable event log or snapshots for recovery.

options.title: SQL ranking table
options.description: Store user scores in a relational table and compute ranks with ordered queries.
tradeoffs: Rank queries become expensive at large N.

flows.name: Score update
flows.note: The API writes a durable event and increments the sorted set.
diagram: participant R as Redis Sorted Set

deepDives: Redis is not the durable source of truth in this design; the score event log is.
bottlenecks: Single hot sorted set can hit memory or CPU limits for huge games.
Persistence settings affect recovery point objective.

steps.title: 4. Serve Top, Rank, and Neighbor Queries
steps.description: Top 10 is a range from the head of the sorted set. User rank is a direct rank lookup. Neighbor results are a small range around the user rank.
steps.decisionPrompt: How are each of the required reads implemented against the active board?

concepts.definition: Fetch the highest scoring K entries.
concepts.whyItMatters: It powers the top 10 leaderboard.

concepts.definition: Find a specific user position.
concepts.whyItMatters: It supports personal progress views.

concepts.definition: Fetch entries around a user rank.
concepts.whyItMatters: It makes the leaderboard feel competitive beyond the top 10.

flows.name: Neighborhood lookup
flows.note: The API first gets the user rank, then queries a bounded range around it.
diagram: participant R as Redis Sorted Set

deepDives: Ties need a policy. Redis can sort equal scores lexicographically unless you encode a tie-breaker or compute shared ranks separately.
bottlenecks: Large profile hydration after rank lookup can dominate latency.
Shared-rank semantics are harder than ordinal ranks.
talkingPoints: Keep profile data out of the sorted set; hydrate names and avatars separately.

steps.title: 5. Add Durability With Event Log and Snapshots
steps.description: Because the active sorted set is optimized for speed, score events and periodic snapshots provide rebuild and recovery after data loss or failover.
steps.decisionPrompt: If Redis loses memory, how do we rebuild the leaderboard accurately?

concepts.definition: Reconstruct state by replaying immutable events.
concepts.whyItMatters: It gives a durable source of truth for scores.

concepts.definition: A point-in-time dump of leaderboard state.
concepts.whyItMatters: It speeds recovery by avoiding replay from the beginning of the month.

concepts.definition: A process that rebuilds sorted sets from snapshots and events.
concepts.whyItMatters: It turns durability into an operational recovery path.

deepDives: Snapshots are a performance optimization for recovery; events remain the audit trail.
bottlenecks: Replay time grows with event volume.
Exactly-once processing is usually implemented through idempotent event IDs rather than magical infrastructure.
talkingPoints: Define recovery point and recovery time objectives.

steps.title: 6. Partition Only When One Sorted Set Is Not Enough
steps.description: A single sorted set can often handle 25 million players. If a tournament grows beyond one node, partitioning by score ranges or hash buckets introduces tradeoffs for global top and rank.
steps.decisionPrompt: When does the leaderboard need sharding, and which queries become harder?

concepts.definition: Shard users by score bands.
concepts.whyItMatters: It makes top queries efficient but needs rebalancing as users move scores.

concepts.definition: Shard users by user ID hash.
concepts.whyItMatters: It balances writes but makes global rank aggregation harder.

concepts.definition: Combining shard-local results into one leaderboard answer.
concepts.whyItMatters: It is required once the active board is split.

deepDives: This step should be conditional. Do not shard before explaining why the single sorted set is insufficient.
bottlenecks: Global rank across hash shards is expensive.
Score-range shards can become imbalanced at the top.
talkingPoints: For the given numbers, a single Redis sorted set may be a valid first design with replicas and snapshots.

steps.title: 7. Define Tie-Breaking and Fairness Rules
steps.description: The prompt says ties share the same rank unless a tie-breaker is added. The API and display logic must make that policy explicit.
steps.decisionPrompt: How should two players with the same score be ranked and displayed?

concepts.definition: Players with equal scores display the same rank.
concepts.whyItMatters: It matches many tournament expectations but requires extra rank computation.

concepts.definition: A secondary ordering such as earliest score time or user ID.
concepts.whyItMatters: It gives stable ordering but may not display shared ranks.

concepts.definition: A product rule that defines how rankings are perceived by players.
concepts.whyItMatters: Leaderboard correctness includes user trust, not only data structures.

deepDives: If shared ranks are mandatory, you may need additional counts by score or a query pattern that computes rank as number of players with strictly higher score plus one.
bottlenecks: Shared-rank computation can be slower than ordinal ZREVRANK.
Tie policies must remain stable during the tournament.
talkingPoints: State the simpler default and the exact alternative if asked.

steps.title: 8. Close With Failure Recovery and Operations
steps.description: Replicas, snapshots, event logs, metrics, and alerting make the leaderboard reliable during Redis failures, monthly rollovers, and game launch spikes.
steps.decisionPrompt: What are the operational risks during a tournament?

concepts.definition: A copy used to scale reads or fail over.
concepts.whyItMatters: It improves availability for top and rank queries.

concepts.definition: A scheduled job that opens the next tournament namespace.
concepts.whyItMatters: It turns reset into a predictable operation.

concepts.definition: A measure of delay between score event creation and leaderboard visibility.
concepts.whyItMatters: It tells whether the board is still real time enough.

deepDives: Monthly reset should be tested like a production migration, not treated as a cron afterthought.
bottlenecks: Failover can briefly serve stale ranks.
Launch spikes can overwhelm profile hydration even if Redis is fine.
talkingPoints: End with score write path, top read path, and rebuild path.

functional.how: Trusted game services send idempotent score events, which are logged durably and applied to the active monthly Redis sorted set.
Sorted-set range and rank commands serve top, user rank, and neighborhood reads without scanning the full board.
Monthly tournament namespaces and a current alias make reset a rollover operation while preserving historical boards.
The event log and periodic snapshots rebuild active sorted sets after cache or node loss.
Read replicas serve hot rank queries first; shard routing and aggregation are introduced only if one active board no longer fits.

nonFunctional.how: Score events update the active sorted set synchronously after durable append, so reads see changes quickly.
Snapshots plus event replay define the recovery path and keep Redis from being the only source of truth.
Tie policy and rank semantics are explicit so players understand equal scores and ordering behavior.

finalDesign.title: Final Design
finalDesign.description: The final design uses a durable score-event log plus a hot monthly Redis sorted set. Game services emit idempotent match-win events, the leaderboard API appends events and increments the active tournament sorted set, read APIs serve top, rank, and neighborhood queries, snapshots and replay rebuild Redis, and monthly rollover creates a new namespace instead of destructive reset.
finalDesign.decisionPrompt: Walk through a score update and a nearby-rank read, then explain how the board recovers if Redis is lost.

concepts.term: Hot serving state plus durable log
concepts.definition: Redis serves ranks, while the event log is the source of truth.
concepts.whyItMatters: It combines low latency with recoverability.

concepts.definition: Each month has its own leaderboard key.
concepts.whyItMatters: It makes reset a controlled rollover rather than a mass delete.

concepts.definition: Ordinal rank and shared rank are different product choices.
concepts.whyItMatters: Tie handling affects both correctness and query cost.

diagram: API -.->|only if one set is too large| PartitionRouter

options.title: Full Final Design
options.description: Complete architecture combining every major step from the interview. This is the default wrap-up view.
diagram: API -.->|only if one set is too large| PartitionRouter

options.title: Sharded mega-tournament variant
options.description: Use this only when one sorted set no longer fits memory or CPU. It makes global rank and ties more complex.

flows.name: Score update
flows.note: A score update is idempotently logged, applied to the active monthly sorted set, and can return the updated rank.
diagram: participant R as Redis Sorted Set

flows.name: Rebuild after cache loss
flows.note: Snapshots reduce recovery time, while events after the snapshot restore exact state.
diagram: participant R as Redis Sorted Set

deepDives: Redis is an implementation detail for active rank operations; the durable event log and snapshot path make it a reliable system design.
bottlenecks: Profile hydration can dominate read latency.
Shared-rank computation can be harder than ordinal rank.
Shard aggregation is complex and should be introduced only after sizing proves it is needed.
talkingPoints: Start with one sorted set for the prompt scale.
Use game service as the trusted score source.
Treat monthly rollover as a namespace change.

followUps: How would you compute shared ranks efficiently?
How would you prevent cheating or duplicate match events?
When would you shard the sorted set?
How do you rebuild after losing Redis primary and replica?
